name: Participant Recruitment Pathways
consequences: Pulling this lever changes how participants are sourced, affecting enrollment speed, sample representativeness, and cost per participant. Expanding outreach to community sleep clinics increases reach but may dilute diagnostic certainty, requiring additional screening. Targeted advertising in sleep disorder forums accelerates enrollment but risks attracting self-selected participants with atypical symptom profiles, potentially biasing event yield. A structured referral program with neurologists maintains diagnostic rigor but limits volume, extending the recruitment timeline.
options: Launch a structured referral program with regional neurologists, ensuring high diagnostic certainty while maintaining controlled enrollment volume.
Deploy targeted online advertising in sleep disorder forums and social media groups, rapidly increasing participant awareness and accelerating enrollment.
Partner with community sleep clinics to conduct on-site screening events, broadening reach but requiring additional logistical coordination.
review: Expanding recruitment channels increases enrollment speed but introduces variability in diagnostic rigor, and the options do not address how to standardize screening quality across disparate sites.

name: Night Shift Staffing Architecture
consequences: Pulling this lever determines coverage reliability, staff fatigue, and cost. A rotating shift schedule maximizes coverage but may increase turnover and reduce continuity of care. Dedicated full-time technicians improve consistency but raise salary and insurance costs. Remote monitoring support reduces on-site staffing but relies on reliable connectivity and may delay real-time event response, potentially compromising safety.
options: Implement a rotating shift schedule for technicians, ensuring continuous coverage while allowing flexible work-life balance for staff.
Hire dedicated full-time technicians for each suite, enhancing continuity of care and reducing handover errors during night shifts.
Deploy remote monitoring support via secure video links, minimizing on-site staff while maintaining real-time event detection and response.
review: Choosing a staffing architecture balances coverage reliability against cost and staff fatigue, yet the options overlook mechanisms for ensuring consistent training and quality assurance across all technicians.

name: Sensor Acquisition Strategy
consequences: Pulling this lever affects data resolution, participant burden, and event capture accuracy. Dry-electrode headbands reduce setup time and improve participant comfort but may lower signal fidelity, potentially missing subtle arousals. Full PSG nights provide gold-standard data but increase cost, time, and participant fatigue, limiting nightly coverage. A hybrid sensor integration balances breadth and depth but requires complex synchronization and may introduce data integration challenges.
options: Prioritize dry-electrode headband monitoring for all nights, streamlining setup and enhancing participant comfort while accepting modest signal trade-offs.
Schedule full PSG montages on every third night, ensuring high-resolution data for periodic validation while maintaining overall coverage.
Integrate contact-free mattress sensors with infrared cameras for continuous environmental context, requiring sophisticated data fusion but expanding event detection cues.
review: Adjusting sensor acquisition balances participant comfort against data fidelity, yet the options fail to address how to validate signal quality across diverse sensor modalities.

name: Event Detection and Triage Framework
consequences: Pulling this lever determines manual review workload, detection sensitivity, and false-positive rates. A rule-based threshold system offers transparency but may miss complex patterns, increasing manual oversight. A machine learning model can capture nuanced events but requires extensive labeled data and may produce opaque decisions. A hybrid human-in-the-loop approach blends algorithmic speed with expert judgment, potentially reducing false positives but increasing coordination overhead.
options: Deploy a rule-based threshold system that flags events based on amplitude and duration, providing clear criteria for technician review.
Train a supervised machine learning model on annotated PSG data, enabling automated detection of subtle parasomnia signatures across nights.
Implement a human-in-the-loop workflow where the algorithm pre-screens events and technicians confirm or reject, balancing speed and accuracy.
review: Choosing an event triage framework trades transparency for speed, and the options do not address how to maintain consistent adjudication standards across technicians.

name: Residential Environment Design
consequences: Pulling this lever influences participant comfort, data quality, and safety compliance. A fully domestic aesthetic reduces participant anxiety but may compromise acoustic isolation, increasing ambient noise artifacts. A clinical environment enhances safety monitoring but may suppress naturalistic behavior, reducing event capture fidelity. An adaptive environment with dynamic lighting and sound masking can balance safety and naturalistic behavior but requires additional investment in smart controls and staff training.
options: Maintain a domestic interior with padded corridors and acoustic treatment, prioritizing naturalistic behavior while ensuring basic safety compliance.
Convert suites into clinical-style rooms with dedicated monitoring stations, maximizing safety oversight but potentially dampening participant spontaneity.
Install adaptive lighting and sound masking systems that adjust to participant activity, balancing safety with a relaxed residential feel.
review: Designing the residential environment balances participant comfort against safety and data integrity, yet the options do not specify how to monitor and adjust acoustic noise levels in real time.

name: Capital Expenditure Prioritization
consequences: Pulling this lever determines resource distribution across equipment, staffing, and participant incentives. Allocating more to equipment enhances data quality but reduces available funds for staff and participant compensation, potentially slowing enrollment. Prioritizing staff salaries improves coverage and reduces turnover but may limit high-end sensor acquisition, compromising event detection fidelity. Increasing participant compensation boosts enrollment speed but may inflate per-participant cost, affecting overall budget sustainability.
options: Allocate a larger share of the budget to advanced sensor hardware, ensuring high-resolution data while scaling back on participant incentives.
Prioritize staff salary increases to reduce turnover, accepting a modest reduction in equipment upgrades to maintain operational stability.
Boost participant compensation to accelerate enrollment, while reallocating a portion of the equipment budget to maintain essential monitoring capabilities.
review: Reprioritizing capital expenditures trades data fidelity against staffing stability, and the options do not address how to safeguard against budget overruns during the pilot phase.

name: Participant Retention Incentive Structure
consequences: Increasing the incentive structure raises participant motivation and may extend study duration per individual, but the added financial outlay could strain the budget and risk introducing bias if participants modify behavior to maximize rewards. The higher cost per participant may reduce the number of recruits that can be supported within the fixed budget, potentially limiting statistical power.
options: Offer tiered financial compensation tied to study milestones, such as completion of baseline assessment, mid‑study monitoring, and final debrief.
Provide personalized sleep health reports and follow‑up consultations to participants, reinforcing engagement and offering actionable insights.
Create a community support group with peer mentorship and shared resources, enabling participants to discuss experiences and coping strategies.
review: Higher incentives raise costs and risk behavior bias, yet the options fail to address long‑term participant engagement beyond the study period.

name: Sleep Suite Utilization Optimization
consequences: Optimizing suite utilization can increase throughput and reduce idle nights, but it may elevate noise exposure for participants and strain technicians if schedules become too tight. The tighter shift alignment could also reduce flexibility for emergency event response, potentially compromising safety.
options: Implement staggered admission windows to balance night shift load, ensuring technicians cover peak event periods without overtime.
Introduce flexible suite sharing for participants with similar sleep patterns, allowing simultaneous monitoring while maintaining individual privacy.
Deploy automated room assignment algorithms based on predicted event likelihood, optimizing suite utilization and reducing idle time.
review: Tighter suite scheduling improves throughput but increases staffing strain and noise exposure, while the options do not mitigate technician fatigue during peak event periods.

name: Data Annotation Workflow Efficiency
consequences: Streamlining annotation speeds up data processing and lessens scorer fatigue, yet shortcuts may lower inter‑rater reliability if preliminary marks are not thoroughly vetted. Reduced reliability could inflate false positives in event detection, undermining the validity of downstream analyses.
options: Adopt a tiered annotation protocol where technicians perform preliminary marks before expert reviewers finalize scoring, speeding workflow.
Integrate real‑time confidence scoring to flag ambiguous epochs for focused review, allowing raters to prioritize high‑uncertainty segments.
Use a collaborative annotation platform that allows simultaneous multi‑rater editing with version control, ensuring traceability of changes.
review: Speeding annotation reduces turnaround but risks lower reliability, which could compromise event‑triage accuracy.

name: Ethics Compliance Cadence
consequences: Frequent ethics reviews reinforce compliance and participant protection, but the administrative load may slow recruitment and increase staff time spent on documentation. The slower pace could delay data collection milestones and extend the overall project timeline.
options: Schedule quarterly ethics board reviews with pre‑submitted progress summaries, ensuring timely feedback while limiting disruption to recruitment.
Implement continuous monitoring via an internal compliance dashboard that tracks protocol adherence in real time, alerting staff to deviations.
Engage external auditors to conduct surprise site visits, providing independent verification of safety and data integrity standards.
review: Frequent ethics reviews increase compliance but add administrative overhead, yet the options do not improve participant understanding of consent, which may affect informed decision‑making.

name: Funding Diversification Strategy
consequences: Diversifying funding sources can stabilize cash flow and reduce dependence on a single grant, but it introduces additional reporting obligations and potential conflicts of interest. The increased administrative burden may divert resources from core research activities.
options: Pursue additional EU Horizon grants targeting rare sleep disorders, expanding funding streams while aligning with European research priorities.
Develop a small‑scale educational partnership with local universities for student research projects, leveraging academic talent and reducing staffing costs.
Offer limited‑access data services to non‑profit sleep research consortia under strict data use agreements, generating revenue without compromising privacy.
review: Broadening funding reduces financial risk but adds reporting complexity, and the options do not secure long‑term institutional backing without establishing a dedicated endowment or university partnership.

name: Interdisciplinary Collaboration Model
consequences: Enhancing cross‑disciplinary input can accelerate innovation and improve algorithm relevance, but it may dilute focus and create coordination overhead. The added meetings and shared decision‑making could delay protocol finalization and extend the development cycle.
options: Establish a monthly interdisciplinary workshop with neurologists, engineers, and data scientists, fostering cross‑disciplinary dialogue and rapid prototyping.
Create a joint task force to co‑design event‑triage algorithms, ensuring clinical relevance and technical feasibility from the outset.
Implement a shared research portal where collaborators can submit and review protocol amendments, streamlining approvals and maintaining version history.
review: Cross‑disciplinary workshops accelerate innovation but create coordination overhead, while the options fail to align all stakeholders on a unified research agenda.

name: Community Outreach and Referral Network Expansion
consequences: Expanding outreach to community sleep clinics and patient advocacy groups increases recruitment diversity and volume, but may dilute screening rigor and increase pre‑screening workload. Downstream: higher enrollment may strain staffing and resources, potentially impacting data quality if not matched with capacity.
options: Launch a targeted outreach campaign to regional sleep centers, offering joint case reviews and referral incentives to increase qualified participant flow.
Partner with national sleep disorder patient advocacy groups to host informational webinars, enabling self-referral and broader demographic reach.
Deploy a mobile screening unit to community health fairs, providing on‑site pre‑screening and immediate referral pathways to the Bonn unit.
review: Expanding outreach raises recruitment volume but adds pre‑screening burden and may lower screening precision; the options do not address how to maintain screening rigor while scaling.

name: Adaptive Night Shift Staffing Algorithm
consequences: Implementing a data‑driven staffing algorithm that reallocates technicians based on predicted event density improves response times and reduces idle hours, but introduces complexity in shift planning and may increase overtime costs. Downstream: better coverage could improve event capture but may reduce technician morale if schedules become unpredictable.
options: Develop a predictive model using historical event rates to schedule technicians during peak event windows, ensuring coverage aligns with expected activity.
Introduce a flexible shift rotation system where technicians self‑select coverage slots based on personal availability and predicted workload.
Implement a real‑time monitoring dashboard that alerts supervisors to staffing gaps, allowing rapid reallocation of technicians during high‑activity periods.
review: The algorithm enhances coverage but adds scheduling complexity and potential overtime; the options fail to balance technician workload stability with dynamic allocation.

name: Sensor Calibration and Drift Mitigation Cycle
consequences: Instituting a regular calibration schedule for all sensors maintains signal fidelity and reduces false positives, but requires downtime and may interrupt participant nights, potentially affecting data continuity. Downstream: improved data quality may reduce annotation effort but could increase operational costs.
options: Schedule weekly calibration sessions during scheduled participant downtimes, aligning sensor checks with planned room turnovers.
Deploy automated self‑calibration routines embedded in sensor firmware, allowing continuous drift correction without manual intervention.
Use a redundant sensor array strategy, where duplicate sensors cross‑validate data, flagging drift before it impacts recordings.
review: Regular calibration improves data quality but disrupts participant nights; the options do not address how to minimize participant inconvenience while ensuring calibration.

name: Automated Event Flagging and Escalation Workflow
consequences: Automating initial event flagging with lightweight algorithms reduces technician review load, but may miss subtle events or generate false alarms, requiring manual verification and potentially increasing annotation time. Downstream: faster triage could accelerate data analysis but may compromise event detection sensitivity.
options: Integrate a rule‑based threshold system that flags rapid arousal patterns, prompting technicians to review only high‑probability episodes.
Employ a lightweight machine learning model trained on pilot data to rank events by likelihood, allowing technicians to focus on top‑tier candidates.
Combine sensor modalities into a multimodal scoring system that flags events only when EEG, motion, and acoustic cues co‑occur, reducing false positives.
review: Automation cuts review time but risks missing events; the options do not address how to validate algorithm accuracy against human scoring.

name: Participant Experience and Comfort Optimization
consequences: Enhancing participant comfort through environmental tweaks (lighting, noise control, personalized amenities) increases retention and data quality, but may raise renovation costs and complicate infection control protocols. Downstream: better sleep quality may reduce artifact rates but could introduce variability in environmental conditions.
options: Install adjustable ambient lighting systems that mimic natural circadian cues, allowing participants to set preferred light levels during sleep.
Provide individualized bedding options and temperature controls, enabling participants to tailor comfort settings to personal preferences.
Introduce a quiet zone policy that limits external noise and enforces strict visitor schedules, ensuring a consistently restful environment.
review: Comfort improvements boost retention but add cost and complexity; the options do not address how to balance personalization with standardization across suites.

name: Longitudinal Data Quality Assurance Protocol
consequences: Implementing a continuous quality assurance protocol that monitors data integrity in real time ensures high‑quality datasets, but requires additional personnel and software infrastructure, potentially diverting resources from core research activities. Downstream: early detection of data loss or corruption can prevent costly re‑collection but may increase administrative overhead.
options: Deploy automated data integrity checks that flag missing or corrupted files immediately, triggering instant technician alerts for corrective action.
Assign a dedicated data steward who reviews nightly data dumps, verifies completeness, and documents quality metrics for each participant.
Integrate a blockchain‑inspired immutable log of data uploads to provide tamper‑evident records, enhancing auditability without manual checks.
review: Continuous QA improves data reliability but adds overhead; the options do not address how to integrate QA without overburdening staff.
